This is a copy of a conversation between ChatGPT & Anonymous.

Report conversation
You said:
. You're an excellent senior Python engineer and developer and a DSA specialist who has almost all problems solved on LeetCode. I want you to teach me each and every pattern necessary to become a senior dev in Python and being able to challenge and solve complex problems easily. I want you to understand that all your outputs will be structured and explained line by line with all the code listed and the problems necessary in order to get me to that level. I want you to tell me what problems are necessary in order to get to that level and also give me each and every problem with line-by-line explanation of each and every line in the code and teach me in order to become an expert Python dev.

You said:
what would one require to build our own agent harness

ChatGPT said:
Thought for 11s

To build your own agent harness, you need the layer that sits around the model and makes it reliable.

An agent harness is not the model itself. It is the runtime that handles:

prompt assembly

tool calling

state management

execution loop

memory/context injection

retries and error handling

logging, tracing, evaluation, and guardrails

Think of it as the orchestrator + runtime + safety rails for agents.

Core pieces you need
1. Model interface

A clean wrapper over the LLM.

It should hide provider-specific details and expose one consistent API like:

Python
class LLMClient:
    def complete(self, messages, tools=None, temperature=0.2):
        ...

This layer should handle:

model name

retries

timeouts

token counting

streaming or non-streaming

provider switching

Without this, everything gets tightly coupled to one API.

2. Message/state schema

You need a standard internal representation for conversations and steps.

Example:

Python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class Message:
    role: str
    content: str

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    name: str
    arguments: dict[str, Any]

@dataclass
class StepResult:
    thought: str | None = None
    tool_calls: list[ToolCall] = field(default_factory=list)
    final_answer: str | None = None

This matters because the harness needs to know:

what the agent has seen

what it has done

what tool outputs came back

whether it should continue or stop

3. Tool registry

Agents become useful when they can call tools.

You need:

a way to register tools

JSON-schema-like argument definitions

execution wrapper

validation

failure handling

Minimal example:

Python
class Tool:
    def __init__(self, name, description, schema, fn):
        self.name = name
        self.description = description
        self.schema = schema
        self.fn = fn

    def run(self, **kwargs):
        return self.fn(**kwargs)

Then a registry:

Python
class ToolRegistry:
    def __init__(self):
        self._tools = {}

    def register(self, tool: Tool):
        self._tools[tool.name] = tool

    def get(self, name: str) -> Tool:
        return self._tools[name]

You also need rules for:

tool timeout

malformed args

unknown tool names

repeated useless tool loops

4. Agent loop

This is the heart of the harness.

Basic loop:

build prompt from system + user + state

call model

inspect output

if tool requested, run tool

append tool result to state

call model again

stop on final answer or max steps

Pseudo-code:

Python
def run_agent(user_input: str, state, llm, tools, max_steps=10):
    state.add_user_message(user_input)

    for _ in range(max_steps):
        response = llm.complete(
            messages=state.to_messages(),
            tools=tools.describe()
        )

        parsed = parse_model_output(response)

        if parsed.final_answer:
            state.add_assistant_message(parsed.final_answer)
            return parsed.final_answer

        for call in parsed.tool_calls:
            tool = tools.get(call.name)
            result = tool.run(**call.arguments)
            state.add_tool_result(call.name, result)

    raise RuntimeError("Agent exceeded max steps")

This is the minimum viable harness.

5. Prompt construction layer

You should not hardcode giant prompt strings everywhere.

You want a prompt builder that combines:

system instructions

tool descriptions

memory/context

user request

scratch state / prior steps

output format instructions

You need this layer because agent quality often depends more on prompt composition than raw model quality.

6. Output parser

The model output must be turned into something executable.

You need to parse:

final answer

tool name

tool arguments

control decisions like continue/stop

This parser must be defensive because models can produce:

invalid JSON

partially correct structures

hallucinated tool names

missing required args

So your harness needs either:

strict structured outputs, or

robust repair/parsing logic

7. Memory/context layer

You need rules for what context goes into each call.

Usually this includes:

current conversation

retrieved documents

prior tool results

task plan

user profile/preferences

summarized long history

Without memory discipline, the context window gets polluted fast.

You usually want:

working memory: current task state

episodic memory: past interactions or summaries

retrieval memory: docs/search results

tool memory: previous tool outputs

8. Stop conditions

A harness needs to know when to stop.

Typical stop conditions:

model produced final answer

max steps reached

repeated identical tool calls

no progress detected

tool failure threshold exceeded

This is one of the biggest differences between a demo agent and a usable one.

9. Observability

You need to see what the agent is doing.

At minimum, log:

prompt sent

model response

tool calls

tool outputs

latency

token usage

errors

final result

For serious use, also track:

per-step traces

cost by request

loop depth

failure reasons

hallucinated tool calls

retry counts

If you cannot inspect behavior, you cannot improve the harness.

10. Evaluation harness

This is what most people skip.

You need a way to test:

does the agent solve tasks correctly?

does it choose the right tools?

does it loop?

does it fail safely?

does it degrade under noisy input?

Useful eval categories:

single-tool tasks

multi-step tasks

malformed tool input

ambiguous user queries

tool outage simulation

adversarial prompt injection

long-context tasks

Without evals, every change becomes guesswork.

What a production-grade harness additionally needs

A serious version also needs:

Guardrails

input validation

output validation

prompt injection defenses

tool permission boundaries

unsafe action confirmation

sandboxing for code/file tools

Reliability

retries with backoff

circuit breakers

fallback models

idempotent tool execution

caching

resumable runs

Performance

parallel tool execution where safe

batching

retrieval caching

prompt compression

context summarization

Multi-agent support

If you want more than one agent, you need:

role definitions

handoff rules

shared state

supervisor logic

conflict resolution

But do not start here. Start with one good agent.

Minimal tech choices for a Python v0

If you want to build it yourself in Python, a clean v0 is:

FastAPI for API layer

Pydantic for schemas

httpx for provider/tool calls

SQLite/Postgres for run storage

Redis optional for queues/cache

plain Python classes for orchestration

structured logging

pytest for evals/tests

You do not need a heavy framework first.

Suggested folder structure
agent_harness/
├── app/
│   ├── api/
│   │   └── routes.py
│   ├── agents/
│   │   ├── runner.py
│   │   ├── prompts.py
│   │   ├── parser.py
│   │   └── state.py
│   ├── llm/
│   │   └── client.py
│   ├── tools/
│   │   ├── registry.py
│   │   ├── base.py
│   │   └── implementations/
│   ├── memory/
│   │   └── store.py
│   ├── evals/
│   │   └── cases.py
│   ├── observability/
│   │   └── tracing.py
│   └── main.py
├── tests/
└── pyproject.toml
What you need intellectually

Beyond code, you need clarity on these design questions:

What kind of agent is this?

chatbot with tools?

researcher?

coding agent?

workflow executor?

customer support agent?

What actions can it take?

Every allowed action creates risk and complexity.

What is the unit of state?

per turn?

per conversation?

per task?

per workflow run?

What does success mean?

correct answer?

correct tool selection?

task completion?

low latency?

low cost?

What are hard constraints?

must not email without approval?

must not write files outside sandbox?

must cite sources?

must ask before external side effects?

Those decisions shape the harness more than code does.

Practical maturity ladder
Level 1: toy harness

one model

one or two tools

one execution loop

max step count

simple logging

Level 2: usable harness

schema-validated tools

retries

persistent state

traces

eval suite

better prompt building

Level 3: serious harness

memory layers

guardrails

task recovery

model routing

async jobs

observability dashboard

offline evaluation pipeline

Biggest mistakes people make

Building multi-agent first
One agent with good tooling beats five vague agents.

No state model
Then debugging becomes impossible.

No tool validation
The model emits junk and your runtime breaks.

No stop conditions
The agent loops forever or burns tokens.

No evaluation set
You cannot tell whether improvements are real.

Overusing frameworks too early
You learn less and lose control of the core loop.

If I were building it from scratch, I would start with this exact sequence

define message and tool schemas

build one LLM client wrapper

build one tool registry

build one synchronous agent loop

add parser + final answer/tool call branching

add max-steps and failure handling

add structured logs and traces

add persistence for runs

write 20 eval tasks

only then add retrieval, memory, and multi-agent features

That is the correct order.

The shortest answer

To build your own agent harness, you need:

an LLM wrapper

a message/state model

a tool registry

an execution loop

prompt construction

output parsing

memory/context handling

stop conditions

logging/tracing

evaluation + guardrails

That is the real minimum.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete Python project scaffold with the exact files and starter code for a minimal but clean agent harness.

Voice
